request.question: What consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom that is located in Warrington, England , it has a park that  is now part of the resort, and there are  three locations opened by Gullivers?

title: Amusement park
American Waterfront (Tokyo DisneySea)
Leofoo Village Theme Park
List of former Hersheypark attractions
Gulliver's Land
Safari World
Scandia Amusements
Legoland Windsor Resort
Gulliver's World
Minnesota Zoo
sentences: An amusement park is a park that features various attractions, such as rides and games, as well as other events for entertainment purposes.
A theme park is a type of amusement park that bases its structures and attractions around a central theme, often featuring multiple areas with different themes.
Unlike temporary and mobile funfairs and carnivals, amusement parks are stationary and built for long-lasting operation.
They are more elaborate than city parks and playgrounds, usually providing attractions that cater to a variety of age groups.
While amusement parks often contain themed areas, theme parks place a heavier focus with more intricately-designed themes that revolve around a particular subject or group of subjects.
American Waterfront is a "port-of-call" (themed land) at Tokyo DisneySea in the Tokyo Disney Resort.

It represents the Northeastern seaboard of the United States in the early 20th century, and features two themed areas, an Old Cape Cod section, and a New York Harbor section with an elaborate backstory.
The Leofoo Village Theme Park () is a theme park and a safari located in Guanxi Township, Hsinchu County, Taiwan.

It features three roller coasters, including an inverted shuttle coaster, Screaming Condor, an Intamin twist-and-turn coaster dubbed Sahara Twist, as well as a Vekoma Roller Skater (335m) in the Wild West section of the park, Little Rattler, themed to an old mining railway.
There is also the Nairobi Express, a narrow gauge railway built by Severn Lamb.
The park features many other attractions of different styles and proper themed areas.
Hersheypark (operating as Hershey Park through 1970) is an amusement park located in Hershey, Derry Township, Pennsylvania.
The park was formally opened by Milton S. Hershey on May 30, 1906, and it became an entity of Hershey Estates when the Estates company was established in 1927.
From its opening in 1906 until 1970, the park was an open gate park.
In 1971, the park was gated and an entry fee charged.
This was the first preparations for the renovation project designed by R. Duell and Associates that would begin in 1972.
Since then, the park grew into the park it is today.
Casualties of these different changes are the rides and attractions that were removed or replaced.
Below is a list of the different rides and attractions Hersheypark has removed, which includes kiddie rides, thrill rides, roller coasters, themed areas, funhouses and other attractions of amusement.
Gulliver's Land is a children's theme park in Milton Keynes, England.
It opened in 1999 and is the third park to be opened by Gulliver's.
(The other two parks are Gulliver's World and Gulliver's Kingdom).
The park has been designed especially for families.
The Adventurers Village is now part of the resort, providing themed accommodation and short break packages.
Gulliver's Land resort includes Splash Zone, NERF Zone, The SFEAR and Dinosaur and Farm Park.
Safari World is a tourist attraction in Bangkok, Thailand that consists of two parks named Marine Park and Safari Park, operated by Safari World Public Limited.
The park was opened in 1988 with a total area of 480 acres for its open zoo and 180 acres for its bird park.

A major renovation to enhance effectiveness of land use began on 17 April 1989 and its total area developed for the leisure park now consists of an open zoo and a marine park on 500 rai (approximately 200 acres) of land.

Scandia Amusements is a chain of Scandinavian-themed family amusement centers in California, including three locations: Scandia Fun Center in Sacramento, Scandia Family Fun Center in Victorville, and Scandia Amusement Park in Ontario.
All three locations are located adjacent to a major freeway, and include attractions such as an arcade, "Baltic Sea Bumper Boats", batting cages, miniature golf, and "Stockholm Raceway".

Scandia Amusement Park in Ontario is the only location to include a full amusement park, including two roller coasters, called the "Scandia Screamer" and "Little Screamer", along with twelve other amusement rides.
Legoland Windsor Resort, also known as Legoland Windsor, is a child-orentated theme park and resort in Windsor, Berkshire in England, themed around the Lego toy system.
The park opened in 1996 on the former Windsor Safari Park site as the second Legoland after Legoland Billund in Denmark.
In common with the other Legolands across the world, the park's attractions consist of a mixture of Lego-themed rides, models, and building workshops.
The park was acquired by Merlin Entertainments in 2005, which now operates the park, with the Lego Group retaining part ownership (30%).
The facilities are mainly targeted at children between three and twelve.
Gulliver's World is a theme park located in Warrington, England.
Gulliver’s World theme park consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom.
The park features rides and attractions that have been designed especially for children between the ages of 2 and 13.
Splash Zone and NERF Zone are two indoor attractions located next to the main theme park.
The Minnesota Zoo (formerly the Minnesota Zoological Garden), is an AZA-accredited zoo in Apple Valley, Minnesota.
When it opened on May 22, 1978<ref name="mnzoo.org/timeline">http://www.mnzoo.org/guests/birthday/index.asp retrieved October 22, 2006</ref> it was fairly revolutionary in its exhibit design.
The zoo, built in a suburbanizing rural area, had more space to house exhibits and was one of the first zoos to organize its animals by their living environment as opposed to their species.
Exhibits are arrayed in six themed areas, including three themed walking trails ranging from one to two miles in length:

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: What consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom that is located in Warrington, England , it has a park that  is now part of the resort, and there are  three locations opened by Gullivers?
hop1_docs: Gulliver's World | Gulliver's World is a theme park located in Warrington, England.  Gulliver’s World theme park consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom.  The park features rides and attractions that have been designed especially for children between the ages of 2 and 13.  Splash Zone and NERF Zone are two indoor attractions located next to the main theme park.

Gulliver's Land | Gulliver's Land is a children's theme park in Milton Keynes, England.  It opened in 1999 and is the third park to be opened by Gulliver's.  (The other two parks are Gulliver's World and Gulliver's Kingdom).  The park has been designed especially for families.  The Adventurers Village is now part of the resort, providing themed accommodation and short break packages.  Gulliver's Land resort includes Splash Zone, NERF Zone, The SFEAR and Dinosaur and Farm Park.

Gulliver's Kingdom | Gulliver's Kingdom (also known as Gulliver's Matlock Bath) is a theme park aimed at children aged 3 to 13 in the Derbyshire town of Matlock Bath, England.  Founded in 1978 by Ray Phillips, it is still owned by the Phillips family and now has sister theme parks; Gulliver's World in Warrington and Gulliver's Land in Milton Keynes, which opened in 1989 and 1999 respectively.  The park was originally created by Ray Phillips as a model village for his young children to enjoy, and it is close to the site of the Victorian Switchback rollercoaster ride (after which the theme park's main rollercoaster was named).

Legoland Windsor Resort | Legoland Windsor Resort, also known as Legoland Windsor, is a child-orentated theme park and resort in Windsor, Berkshire in England, themed around the Lego toy system.  The park opened in 1996 on the former Windsor Safari Park site as the second Legoland after Legoland Billund in Denmark.  In common with the other Legolands across the world, the park's attractions consist of a mixture of Lego-themed rides, models, and building workshops.  The park was acquired by Merlin Entertainments in 2005, which now operates the park, with the Lego Group retaining part ownership (30%).  The facilities are mainly targeted at children between three and twelve.

Safari World | Safari World is a tourist attraction in Bangkok, Thailand that consists of two parks named Marine Park and Safari Park, operated by Safari World Public Limited.  The park was opened in 1988 with a total area of 480 acres for its open zoo and 180 acres for its bird park.  A major renovation to enhance effectiveness of land use began on 17 April 1989 and its total area developed for the leisure park now consists of an open zoo and a marine park on 500 rai (approximately 200 acres) of land.

Lilliput Nunataks | The Lilliput Nunataks ( ) are three nunataks, from 600 to high and trending southeast–northwest, located 3 nmi north of Gulliver Nunatak on the east side of Graham Land, Antarctica.  The nunataks are snow free on their southeast sides.  They were charted by the Falkland Islands Dependencies Survey and photographed from the air by the Ronne Antarctic Research Expedition in 1947.  The name, from Jonathan Swift's "Gulliver's Travels", means land of small people and was applied by the UK Antarctic Place-Names Committee in association with Gulliver Nunatak.

Disney's Fort Wilderness Resort & Campground | Disney's Fort Wilderness Resort & Campground is a themed camping resort located in the Magic Kingdom Resort Area at the Walt Disney World Resort.  It officially opened on November 19, 1971.  The resort is adjacent to Bay Lake and Disney's River Country, a now-defunct water park.  The resort is also located near Disney's Wilderness Lodge.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Gulliver's World — [1] "Gulliver's World is a theme park located in Warrington, England."
- themed areas — [1] "consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom."
- Gulliver's Land — [2] "It opened in 1999 and is the third park to be opened by Gulliver's."
- Adventurers Village / resort — [2] "The Adventurers Village is now part of the resort"
- Gulliver's Kingdom / sister parks — [3] "Gulliver's World in Warrington and Gulliver's Land in Milton Keynes"

Ambiguities:
- Which single park is being asked about: "Gulliver's World" (Warrington) vs "Gulliver's Land" (Milton Keynes). Passages split features across these pages.
- "park that is now part of the resort" is explicitly tied to "The Adventurers Village" on Gulliver's Land [2], not to Gulliver's World [1] in the passages.

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing confirmation whether the Warrington park (Gulliver's World) is the same entity that "has a park that is now part of the resort" (Adventurers Village). The relation "Adventurers Village is part of [which park]'s resort?" is unresolved.
- Need an explicit, single-page statement that ties: (a) the themed areas Lilliput Land/Gully Town/Safari Kingdom, (b) "now part of the resort" park, and (c) the three Gulliver park locations, to one named entity.

Retrieval direction:
- Open the page "Gulliver's World" (exact title) to confirm any resort/Adventurers Village relation and full description.
- Open the page "Gulliver's Land" (exact title) to verify "The Adventurers Village is now part of the resort" context and its relation to other Gulliver parks.
- Open the page "Gulliver's Kingdom" (exact title) to confirm the list of Gulliver parks and opening years.
- If needed, run the query: "Gulliver's parks list 'Gulliver's World' 'Gulliver's Land' 'Gulliver's Kingdom'".
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.
response.hop2_query: Gulliver's World Lilliput Land Gully Town Safari Kingdom Warrington
hop2_docs: Gulliver's World | Gulliver's World is a theme park located in Warrington, England.  Gulliver’s World theme park consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom.  The park features rides and attractions that have been designed especially for children between the ages of 2 and 13.  Splash Zone and NERF Zone are two indoor attractions located next to the main theme park.

Glumdalclitch | Glumdalclitch is the name Gulliver gives his "nurse" in Book II of Jonathan Swift's "Gulliver's Travels".  In Book I, Gulliver travels to the land of Lilliput.  Leaving there, he travels to the land of Brobdingnag.  In Lilliput, Gulliver was a giant, and in Brobdingnag, he is a dwarf, with the proportions reversed.

Gulliver's Kingdom | Gulliver's Kingdom (also known as Gulliver's Matlock Bath) is a theme park aimed at children aged 3 to 13 in the Derbyshire town of Matlock Bath, England.  Founded in 1978 by Ray Phillips, it is still owned by the Phillips family and now has sister theme parks; Gulliver's World in Warrington and Gulliver's Land in Milton Keynes, which opened in 1989 and 1999 respectively.  The park was originally created by Ray Phillips as a model village for his young children to enjoy, and it is close to the site of the Victorian Switchback rollercoaster ride (after which the theme park's main rollercoaster was named).

Lilliput Nunataks | The Lilliput Nunataks ( ) are three nunataks, from 600 to high and trending southeast–northwest, located 3 nmi north of Gulliver Nunatak on the east side of Graham Land, Antarctica.  The nunataks are snow free on their southeast sides.  They were charted by the Falkland Islands Dependencies Survey and photographed from the air by the Ronne Antarctic Research Expedition in 1947.  The name, from Jonathan Swift's "Gulliver's Travels", means land of small people and was applied by the UK Antarctic Place-Names Committee in association with Gulliver Nunatak.

Lilliput and Blefuscu | Lilliput and Blefuscu are two fictional island nations that appear in the first part of the 1726 novel "Gulliver's Travels" by Jonathan Swift.  The two islands are neighbours in the South Indian Ocean, separated by a channel eight hundred yards wide.  Both are inhabited by tiny people who are about .  Both kingdoms are empires, i.e. realms ruled by a self-styled emperor.  The capital of Lilliput is Mildendo.

Gulliver's Land | Gulliver's Land is a children's theme park in Milton Keynes, England.  It opened in 1999 and is the third park to be opened by Gulliver's.  (The other two parks are Gulliver's World and Gulliver's Kingdom).  The park has been designed especially for families.  The Adventurers Village is now part of the resort, providing themed accommodation and short break packages.  Gulliver's Land resort includes Splash Zone, NERF Zone, The SFEAR and Dinosaur and Farm Park.

Lough Ennell | Lough Ennell (Irish: "Loch Ainninn" ) is a lake near the town of Mullingar, County Westmeath, Ireland.  It is situated beside the N52 road, off the Mullingar/Kilbeggan road.  It is approximately 4.5 mi long by 2 mi wide, with an area of about 3540 acre .  It has a huge area of shallow water with nearly two-thirds of its area being less than 25 feet (8 m) and almost half of it being under 10 ft deep.  Lough Ennell produced Ireland's largest ever lake brown trout at 26 lbs (11.8 kg).  Lough Ennell is dotted with islands, many of which have now become attached to the shoreline as the levels of the lake have changed.  The main river flowing into Lough Ennell is the River Brosna, which enters on the Mullingar side of the lake and exits on the opposite side at Lilliput.  Lilliput and Lilliput House were frequently used by Jonathan Swift as a holiday home and place to write, local tradition states that Jonathan Swift was in a boat on the lake when he looked back at Lilliput and noticed how small the people looked at that distance, hence the inspiration for his most famous book "Gulliver's Travels".  Lilliput at the time was called "Nure" however after the publication of Gulliver's Travels locals began to refer to the lakeshore as Lilliput, the name stuck and today the area is known as Lilliput.
response.summary_2: Gulliver's World is a theme park in Warrington, England, that consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom; Gulliver's Land (opened 1999) has The Adventurers Village as part of its resort, and the three Gulliver parks are Gulliver's World, Gulliver's Land and Gulliver's Kingdom.
